title: Levels of organisation — cells to organisms
examTip: The exam asks you to put the levels in order, or give an example at each level. Know the hierarchy and at least two animal examples and one plant example.
intro: Your body has about 37 trillion cells in it. If they all just did their own thing independently, you'd be a blob. The reason you're not is that cells are organised — grouped into teams, forming bigger and bigger structures, until you get a functioning human being.

keyPoints.heading: The five levels — smallest to largest
keyPoints.content: Cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism. That's the chain. Every living thing is built this way — animals and plants. Learn it in order and you won't drop easy marks.

keyPoints.heading: Tissue
keyPoints.content: A tissue is a group of similar cells doing the same job. Muscular tissue contracts. Epithelial tissue lines surfaces. Glandular tissue makes and secretes substances. One type of cell, one shared job — that's a tissue.

keyPoints.heading: Organ
keyPoints.content: An organ is made of several different tissues working together. The heart contains muscular, glandular and epithelial tissue. The stomach does too. A leaf is a plant organ. If it only had one tissue type, it'd just be a tissue — organs always have more than one.

keyPoints.heading: Organ system
keyPoints.content: An organ system is a group of organs with a shared purpose. The digestive system (mouth, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas) is your main example for this topic. Plants have organ systems too — roots, stems and leaves all count as organs in a plant's shoot system.

tables.title: Levels of organisation — with examples
rows: Group of organs with a shared function

1.question: What is a tissue?
options: A single cell with a specialised function
A group of similar cells working together to do the same job
A group of organs that form a complete system
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — similar cells, same job. That's the definition.
1.wrongFeedback: A tissue is a group of similar cells doing the same job — one step up from individual cells, one step below an organ.

3.question: Which of these is an organ system?
3.correctFeedback: Right — the digestive system is a collection of organs all working together. That's an organ system.
3.wrongFeedback: The stomach is an organ. Muscular tissue is a tissue. A muscle cell is a cell. The digestive system is the organ system — multiple organs, one shared function.

commonMistakes: Calling the heart a tissue — it's an organ. It contains multiple tissue types.
Listing only one tissue type when asked about an organ — organs always contain several.
Forgetting plants — roots, stems and leaves are organs; plants have organ systems too.
Getting the order wrong under pressure — write it out: cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
examTips: If asked to put levels in order, go smallest to largest: cell, tissue, organ, organ system, organism.
Always give a specific named example — 'muscular tissue' not just 'tissue'; 'digestive system' not just 'a system'.
For the digestive system, be ready to name at least four organs: stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver.

videoLinks.label: Free Science Lessons — Levels of Organisation

revisionCardBullets: Order (smallest → largest): cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism
Tissue = group of similar cells doing the same job — e.g. muscular tissue, epithelial tissue
Organ = several tissues working together — e.g. heart, stomach, leaf
Organ system = organs with a shared function — e.g. digestive system, circulatory system
The heart contains muscular, glandular and epithelial tissue — it's an organ, not a tissue
Digestive system organs: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, gall bladder, pancreas
Plant organs: leaf, stem, root — plants have organ systems too
